title: Breaking: fail claim stake if already held
description:
## Breaking Change

Currently, staking a claim you already hold extends it. This needs to change:

**New behavior:** Staking a claim that's already held (by anyone, including yourself) should FAIL.

## Rationale

This prevents double-firing hooks. If a hook fires and stakes a claim, another hook trying to stake the same claim should fail rather than silently extend.

## Implementation

1. CLI `bus claims stake` - fail if pattern is already claimed
2. Internal code (hooks, etc.) - fail if pattern is already claimed  
3. Use atomic check-and-stake (append_if) to prevent race conditions

## Affected code

- src/cli/claim.rs - the stake command
- src/core/claim.rs - FileClaim creation/validation
- Anywhere else that creates claims
close_reason: Implemented: claim stake now fails if already held (by anyone). Uses atomic check-and-stake.

title: Send text+attachment as single Telegram message using captions
description:
When a BotBus message has both text and file attachments, the Telegram bridge sends them as separate messages (one sendMessage for text, then sendPhoto/sendDocument for each file). This results in 2+ Telegram messages for what should be 1.

## Expected behavior

A message with text + 1 attachment should be a single Telegram message: the file sent via sendPhoto/sendDocument with the text as the `caption` parameter.

## Current behavior

Text and attachment arrive as separate messages in the Telegram chat.

## Implementation

In publish_message() (src/telegram/service.rs):
1. If message has text + exactly 1 attachment: send the attachment with caption = formatted text (skip separate sendMessage)
2. If message has text + multiple attachments: send first attachment with caption, remaining attachments without caption (or use sendMediaGroup for album)
3. If message has only text (no attachments): sendMessage as before
4. If message has only attachments (no text): send attachments without caption

Note: Telegram captions have a 1024 character limit (vs 4096 for messages). If text exceeds 1024 chars, fall back to separate messages.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Single attachment + text = 1 Telegram message with caption
- [ ] Caption limit (1024 chars) handled gracefully — fall back to 2 messages if too long
- [ ] Multiple attachments handled reasonably (sendMediaGroup or first-with-caption)
- [ ] Messages with no attachments unchanged
- [ ] Messages with no text body still work

title: Review README.md against --help output, add Key Features section
description:
Review README.md and compare it with the actual `--help` output of all subcommands. Fix any discrepancies and add a Key Features section.

## Key Features section should include:

1. **Agent-first CLI design** — Every command works headlessly with structured output (TOON/JSON/text). Designed for AI agents to parse and act on, not just humans to read.

2. **No daemon or server** — Pure CLI with append-only JSONL storage. No background processes, no ports, no setup complexity. Just files on disk.

3. **Built-in TUI** — `bus ui` launches a full terminal UI for humans to monitor agent coordination in real-time.

4. **Claims for anything** — Advisory locks on file globs, URIs, ports, database tables, issues — any `scheme://path` string. Prevents conflicts between concurrent agents.

5. **Hooks** — `bus hooks add` triggers shell commands when messages arrive on channels. Event-driven automation without polling.

6. **Telegram integration** — `bus telegram` runs a headless bridge bot that relays messages between BotBus channels and Telegram chats.

## Commands table gaps (README vs actual CLI):

Missing from commands table:
- `watch` — Stream new messages in real-time
- `subscriptions` — Manage channel subscriptions (add/remove/list)
- `hooks` — Manage channel hooks (add/list/remove/test)
- `statuses` — Manage agent statuses (set/clear/list presence + status message)
- `telegram` — Run the Telegram bridge (headless bot)
- `messages` — Message operations (get by ID)

Possibly stale aliases in README:
- README shows `claim` as top-level, CLI shows `claims stake`
- README shows `check-claim` as top-level, CLI shows `claims check`
- README shows `release` as top-level, CLI shows `claims release`
(Verify whether top-level aliases still work)

## Acceptance criteria:
- [ ] Key Features section added near the top of README (after intro, before Install)
- [ ] Commands table updated with all missing commands
- [ ] Verify claim/check-claim/release aliases and document accurately
- [ ] No features documented that do not actually exist in the CLI

title: bus wait --format json returns text instead of JSON
description:
## Problem

`bus wait --format json` returns human-readable text instead of JSON:

```
$ bus wait --agent test --channel foo --timeout 10 --format json
Waiting for messages in #foo... (timeout: 10s)
✓ Message received in #foo
[16:11] sender: message body
```

## Expected

Should return JSON like the deprecated `--json` flag does:

```
$ bus wait --agent test --channel foo --timeout 10 --json
{
  "received": true,
  "message": {
    "ts": "...",
    "id": "...",
    "agent": "sender",
    "channel": "foo",
    "body": "message body"
  },
  "channel": "foo",
  "reason": "message"
}
```

## Workaround

Use the deprecated `--json` flag instead of `--format json`.

title: Core: Simplify identity module for stateless agents
description:
Simplify the identity module since agents are now stateless.

## Current Implementation (src/core/identity.rs)
- resolve_agent() - checks env var, falls back to state file
- has_identity() - checks if identity exists
- format_export() - formats export command

## New Implementation
- resolve_agent() - just reads BOTBUS_AGENT env var, returns Option<String>
- require_agent() - reads env var, returns Result with helpful error if not set
- Remove state file fallback logic

## Remove
- Any reference to per-project agent state files
- Legacy identity resolution from .botbus/

## Keep
- format_export() - still useful for help messages
- Name validation logic (is_valid_agent_name)

## Example
```rust
pub fn resolve_agent() -> Option<String> {
    std::env::var("BOTBUS_AGENT").ok()
}

pub fn require_agent() -> Result<String> {
    resolve_agent().ok_or_else(|| anyhow!(
        "BOTBUS_AGENT environment variable not set.\n\n\
         Set your identity:\n  \
         export BOTBUS_AGENT=\"
    ))
}
```

description:
## Request

Add `bus send --no-hooks` flag to prevent hooks from firing for a specific message.

## Use case

Some automated messages (status updates, acks, etc.) shouldn't trigger hooks. Currently all sends can fire hooks, which can cause unwanted agent spawns or noise.

## Example

```bash
# This should NOT fire any hooks
bus send --agent myagent mychannel "Status update: processing..." --no-hooks

# This fires hooks normally
bus send --agent myagent mychannel "Review requested @myagent-security"
```

title: Overhaul AGENTS.md: project identity, dedup botbox section, update CLI reference
description:
## Goal

Rewrite AGENTS.md so an agent starts every session as an **expert botbus developer**: deeply familiar with the project, opinionated about scope, resistant to scope creep, and obsessive about agent UX (human UX second). The file loads into context every session — every token must earn its place.

## Current problems

### 1. Stale / incorrect CLI commands

The "Quick Start" and "Agent Communication" sections reference commands that no longer exist:
- `bus claim "src/api/**"` → now `bus claims stake "src/api/**"`
- `bus check-claim src/api/routes.rs` → now `bus claims check src/api/routes.rs`
- `bus release --all` → now `bus claims release --all`
- `bus claim "bead://..."` / `bus check-claim "bead://..."` → same, needs `claims stake` / `claims check`

The "Claims" subsection inside the botbox-managed section uses the correct `bus claims stake/release` syntax, creating an inconsistency.

### 2. Duplication with botbox-managed section

These topics appear twice — once in the hand-written section above and again in the `<!-- botbox:managed-start -->` block:
- **Beads workflow** (hand-written "Beads Workflow Integration" ~50 lines + managed "Beads Quick Reference" table)
- **Claims** (hand-written "Quick Start" claims example + managed "Claims" section)
- **Identity** (hand-written "Quick Start" identity instructions + managed "Identity" section)
- **jj/git workflow** (hand-written "Version Control: jj" ~60 lines + managed "Workspace Quick Reference")
- **Code review / crit** (hand-written "Development Workflow" sections 2–4 + managed "Reviews" section)
- **Commit conventions** appear in the hand-written section but not in managed
- **Release workflow** appears in the hand-written section but not in managed

**Rule**: jj, maw, crit, beads, and release workflow belong exclusively in the botbox-managed section. The hand-written section should cover **botbus-the-project** only.

### 3. Missing project identity / personality

AGENTS.md says nothing about **what botbus is**, why it exists, what its design philosophy is, or what an agent should push back on. A developer agent needs to start the session knowing:
- **What**: Chat-oriented coordination CLI for AI coding agents. Append-only JSONL. No daemon. No server.
- **Why**: Agents working on the same codebase (or across projects) need a simple way to communicate, claim resources, and coordinate — without requiring infrastructure.
- **Design principles**: (1) Zero infrastructure — files on disk, no daemon/server. (2) Agent-first, human-friendly — every command works headlessly with structured output. (3) Claims for anything — not just files, any URI. (4) Append-only — JSONL is the source of truth, indexes are derived. (5) Convention over configuration — sensible defaults, minimal setup.
- **Scope boundaries**: botbus is a coordination primitive. It is NOT a task runner, CI system, deployment tool, or general-purpose message queue. Resist scope creep into: job scheduling, build automation, git operations beyond sync, file editing/patching, or process management.
- **Architecture in one paragraph**: CLI binary (`bus`). Storage is `~/.local/share/botbus/` — channels are `channels/<name>.jsonl`, claims in `claims.jsonl`, state in SQLite indexes (derived, rebuildable). Telegram bridge runs as a long-lived process. TUI is a separate mode (`bus ui`).

### 4. CLI reference is incomplete/stale

The AGENTS.md has no concise command reference. The README has one but it is also slightly stale (missing: `messages`, `subscriptions`, `statuses`, `hooks`, `sync`, `index`, `agentsmd`, `telegram`). The AGENTS.md should have a compact but **complete** command reference with actual current syntax, since agents will copy-paste from it.

Full current command tree (from `bus --help`):
```
bus init, doctor, generate-name, whoami
bus send <target> <message> [-L label] [--attach file] [--no-hooks]
bus history [channel] [-n count] [-f/--follow] [--since/--before] [--from] [-L label]
bus watch [channel]
bus inbox [-c channels] [--all] [--mentions] [-n count] [--mark-read] [--count-only]
bus mark-read <channel>
bus search <query> [-c channel] [-n count] [--from]
bus wait [-c channel] [--mention] [-L label] [-t timeout]
bus agents [--active]
bus channels list|close|reopen|delete|rename
bus claims stake|refresh|release|list|check
bus hooks add|list|remove|test
bus subscriptions add|remove|list
bus statuses set|clear|list
bus messages get <id>
bus status
bus sync init|push|pull|status|log|check
bus index rebuild|status
bus telegram
bus ui [-c channel]
bus agentsmd
```

### 5. Token waste

At 611 lines, AGENTS.md is bloated. Things that can be moved to `.agents/` linked docs:
- **ast-grep vs ripgrep** section (~50 lines) — generic tooling guidance, not botbus-specific. Move to `.agents/search-tools.md`.
- **Testing** section (~45 lines) — important for contributors but not needed in every session context. Move to `.agents/testing.md`.
- **TUI Screenshot** section (~10 lines) — rarely needed. Move to `.agents/tui-screenshot.md`.
- **bv as AI sidecar** section (~15 lines) — useful but niche. Could stay or move to `.agents/bv-sidecar.md`.

### 6. !flags not documented

Messages support inline flags: `!nohooks`, `!nochanhooks`, `!noathooks`. These are useful for agents sending messages that shouldnt trigger automation. Not documented anywhere in AGENTS.md.

### 7. Attachments not documented

`bus send --attach <file>` and `bus send --attach <url>` are not mentioned. Content-addressed storage, deduplication, and the Telegram bridge relay are not documented.

## Proposed structure

```
# botbus

## Project Identity (NEW — ~20 lines)
What, why, design principles, scope boundaries, architecture summary.

## Pre-commit Checks (~5 lines, condensed from current)

## CLI Reference (NEW — ~40 lines)
Complete command tree with current syntax. Compact table format.
Include !flags, --attach, --no-hooks, --format.

## Agent Communication (~30 lines, updated from current)
Quick start with CORRECT commands (claims stake/check/release).
Channel/message conventions (condensed).

## Development Notes (~15 lines, condensed)
Key pointers: identity, storage paths, JSONL append-only model.
Links to .agents/ docs for testing, search tools, TUI screenshots.

## Tools (~25 lines)
Beads essentials (condensed, no duplication with managed section).
bv sidecar (condensed or linked).
Link to .agents/cli-output.md.

## Botbox Workflow (managed section — untouched)
```

Target: ~250 lines (down from 611), every line earning its context-window real estate.

## Acceptance criteria

1. All CLI examples use correct current syntax (`bus claims stake/check/release`, not `bus claim/check-claim/release`)
2. No duplication between hand-written and botbox-managed sections
3. Project identity section exists with design principles and scope boundaries
4. Complete CLI command reference matching `bus --help` output
5. !flags and --attach documented
6. ast-grep, testing, TUI screenshot sections moved to `.agents/` with links
7. Total line count under 300 (excluding botbox-managed section)
8. Builds pass (`cargo fmt && cargo clippy -- -D warnings && cargo test`)
close_reason: done

title: channels rename: update Telegram bridge config
description:
When `bus channels rename old new` runs, the Telegram bridge config should be updated if the channel has a topic mapping.

## Context
Telegram config at `~/.cache/botbus/telegram.json` (telegram/config.rs:17-30) contains:
- `channel_topics: HashMap<String, i64>` — maps botbus channel names to Telegram topic IDs
- `topic_titles: HashMap<i64, String>` — reverse mapping (keyed by topic ID, no change needed)

After rename, the bridge can't find the mapped channel and messages stop bridging.

## Implementation
In `channels.rs:rename()`, after other updates:
1. Check if telegram config exists (it's optional — not all deployments use Telegram)
2. If it exists, load TelegramConfig
3. If `channel_topics` contains the old channel name, move the entry to the new name
4. Save config
5. Print `✓ Updated Telegram mapping` (or skip if no mapping found)

This is lower priority (P3) since Telegram bridge is optional and not all channels are mapped.

## Files
- `src/cli/channels.rs:320-415` — rename() function
- `src/telegram/config.rs:17-30` — TelegramConfig struct
- `src/telegram/config.rs:102-164` — save/load methods

## Testing
- Best tested manually with Telegram bridge running
- Unit test: create config with mapping, rename, verify mapping updated
close_reason: done

title: Epic: Migrate BotBus to global XDG-based storage
description:
Migrate BotBus from per-project .botbus/ directories to a global ~/.local/share/botbus/ installation.

## Motivation
- Per-project storage breaks with git worktrees/workspaces (causes merge conflicts)
- No cross-project communication possible
- Requires manual copy-paste to coordinate agents across projects

## New Architecture
- Single global storage at $XDG_DATA_HOME/botbus/ (~/.local/share/botbus/)
- Channels are global, scoped by naming convention (e.g., #projectname, #projectname.topic)
- Claims use absolute paths (auto-expanded from cwd)
- Agent identity is global and persistent

## Breaking Changes
- Remove botbus init command (or repurpose for project-specific setup)
- Remove --project flag from all commands
- Claims now require absolute paths internally
- Channels are no longer project-scoped by directory

## Version
Bump to 0.2.0
close_reason: XDG migration complete

title: Implement BotBus ↔ Telegram attachments bridge
description:
Implement bidirectional attachment handling between BotBus and Telegram with unified content-addressed storage.

## Overview

Enable file attachments to flow between BotBus messages and Telegram in both directions, using a content-addressed cache with sidecar metadata.

## Design Document

See: notes/attachments.md

## Key Features

1. **Content-addressed storage**: Files stored by SHA256 hash in ~/.local/share/botbus/attachments/
2. **Sidecar metadata**: Each file has .meta.json with Telegram file_id, MIME type, source info
3. **Daemon-managed downloads**: Telegram daemon handles all downloads before publishing messages
4. **MIME detection**: Use infer crate for magic-number detection (not extension-based)
5. **Unified storage**: Same cache for Telegram downloads, CLI bus send --attach, agent APIs
6. **Automatic deduplication**: Same content = same hash = stored once

## Implementation Phases

### Phase 1: Foundation - Cache Infrastructure
- Create src/attachments/ module
- Implement AttachmentCache with content-addressed storage
- Add AttachmentMetadata struct and JSON serialization
- Implement atomic file writes and cleanup utilities
- Unit tests

### Phase 2: Telegram Downloads (Telegram → Bus)
- Extend TelegramClient::download_file()
- Add MIME detection via infer crate
- Update handle_update() to extract media
- Integrate AttachmentCache in telegram daemon
- Create Attachment::File references with cache paths

### Phase 3: Telegram Uploads (Bus → Telegram)
- Add send_photo(), send_video(), send_audio(), send_document() to TelegramClient
- Update publish_message() to send attachments
- Route to appropriate method based on MIME type
- Handle inline/URL attachments

### Phase 4: CLI Integration
- Add --attach flag to bus send command
- Use same AttachmentCache as daemon
- Support multiple attachments per message

### Phase 5: Testing & Polish
- End-to-end tests
- Integration tests for cache cleanup

title: Check claim availability before firing hook
description:
## Problem

When multiple hooks with the same claim_owner fire for the same message (e.g., channel hook + mention hook both spawn maw-dev), both processes spawn even though only one can claim the agent lease.

Current flow:
1. Both hooks fire → botty spawns two processes
2. Both processes start
3. Both try to claim agent://maw-dev
4. One wins, one exits

This works but wastes a spawn.

## Solution

Check claim availability before firing the hook:
- If claim_owner is set, check if `agent://<claim_owner>` is already claimed
- If claimed, skip firing this hook
- If available, fire the hook (process will claim it)

This prevents wasteful duplicate spawns when hooks overlap.

## Alternative

Could also be implemented in botty at spawn time rather than in botbus hook processing.
close_reason: Implemented atomic check-and-stake for hook claims using append_if

title: Support URI-based claims for non-file resources
description:
## Summary

Extend claims beyond file paths to support resource URIs - any addressable thing an agent might want exclusive access to (beads, database tables, API endpoints, etc.).

## Motivation

Currently claims only work for files. But agents coordinate around more than files:
- Multiple agents might work on related issues
- An agent doing a database migration wants to "lock" certain tables
- An agent spinning up a dev server wants to claim a port

## Proposed Design

### URI Schemes

```
file:///path/to/file.rs        # Explicit file (optional)
bead://project/bd-xxx          # A specific issue  
db://myapp/users               # A database table
port://8080                    # A local port
```

### Usage

```bash
# Claim a bead
botbus claim "bead://botbus/bd-111" -m "Working on this issue"

# Claim with wildcard
botbus claim "bead://botbus/*" -m "Major refactor"

# Check before working
botbus check-claim "bead://botbus/bd-111"
```

### Implementation Notes

1. Claims are opaque strings - BotBus doesn't interpret URI semantics
2. Conflict detection uses prefix/glob matching (same as files)
3. Bare paths (no scheme) remain files for backward compatibility
4. Display groups claims by scheme in `botbus claims` output

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `botbus claim` accepts URI strings (not just file paths)
- [ ] `botbus check-claim` works with URIs
- [ ] `botbus claims` displays URIs nicely (grouped by scheme)
- [ ] Backward compatible - bare paths still work as files
- [ ] Documentation updated

## Out of Scope (for now)

- Validating URI schemes
- Integrating with external tools (e.g., checking if bead exists)
- Auto-prefixing file paths with file://
close_reason: Implemented URI claims feature

title: Zombie processes: spawned child processes not reaped
description:
bus ui and bus telegram spawn botty CLI subprocesses but don't call waitpid() on them after they exit, leaving zombie (defunct) processes in the process table.

## Evidence

```
$ ps -eo pid,ppid,stat,tty,args | grep 'botty.*defunct'
 150690  150526 Z+   pts/27   [botty] <defunct>
 152324  150526 Z+   pts/27   [botty] <defunct>
 ...22 total zombies...

$ ps -o pid,ppid,stat,tty,args -p 150526 3739843
 150526 1613322 Sl+  pts/27   bus ui
3739843 3571390 Sl+  pts/49   bus telegram
```

All zombie botty processes are children of bus processes, not the botty server. The bus commands spawn botty CLI subprocesses (e.g. botty spawn, botty list) but never reap them.

## Impact
- PID table entries accumulate over time (22 zombies after ~1 day)
- Harmless individually but will grow unbounded in long-running sessions

## Suggested fix
When spawning child processes, either:
1. Call .wait() on the Child handle after the subprocess completes
2. Use a SIGCHLD handler to reap children asynchronously
3. Double-fork so children reparent to init (which auto-reaps)
close_reason: Completed - fix merged

title: Design: Agent naming and registration conventions
description:
Design the agent naming system for global BotBus.

## Decision: No Registration, Stateless Identity

BotBus is stateless about agent identity. It trusts whatever name is passed via BOTBUS_AGENT env var.

## Key Principles
1. Agent identity is like an IRC nick - stable, not role-based
2. An agent might work on any project, any task - name doesn't imply role
3. Orchestrator/user is responsible for name persistence across restarts
4. BotBus just tags messages/claims with the provided name

## Implementation

### Required
- `BOTBUS_AGENT` env var must be set for all commands that need identity
- Error with helpful message if not set

### Commands
- `botbus generate-name` - Returns random kebab-case name, no side effects
- `botbus whoami` - Prints $BOTBUS_AGENT or error if not set
- `botbus agents` - Derives agent list from message/claim history (no agents.jsonl)

### Removed
- `botbus register` - no registration ceremony
- `agents.jsonl` - no central agent registry
- Join messages - no 'X has joined' announcements

## Name Format: kebab-case (IRC style)
Examples:
- crimson-fox
- silent-oak  
- swift-raven
- quiet-badger

NOT:
- CrimsonFox (PascalCase)
- crimson_fox (snake_case)
- CRIMSON-FOX (SCREAMING)

## Validation
- Lowercase alphanumeric + hyphens
- 1-64 chars
- Must start with letter
- No consecutive hyphens
- No leading/trailing hyphens

## Name Generation
Update names crate usage or generate manually:
```rust
fn generate_name() -> String {
    let adj = random_adjective();  // "crimson", "silent", "swift"
    let noun = random_noun();       // "fox", "oak", "raven"
    format!("{}-{}", adj, noun)
}
```

## Example Workflow
```bash
# Orchestrator spawns agent with stable identity
export BOTBUS_AGENT=$(botbus generate-name)  # -> quiet-river
# Orchestrator persists this name for restarts

# Agent works across projects freely
botbus send botbus 'Picking up bd-123'
botbus send botcrit 'Done with botbus, moving here'

# Looks like IRC:
# <quiet-river> Picking up bd-123
# <swift-raven> On it, will review when done
```
close_reason: Implemented: stateless identity model with BOTBUS_AGENT env var, generate-name command, kebab-case names

title: Inbox --mark-read should indicate remaining unread messages (CLI pagination)
description:
`bus inbox --mark-read` has a message limit (default 50). When there are more unread messages than the limit, the user must call it multiple times to clear the backlog. This is essentially CLI pagination but nothing in the output indicates that there are more messages remaining.

## Problem

If an agent has 200 unread messages and runs `bus inbox --all --mark-read`, it gets 50 messages and marks those as read. But the output gives no indication that 150 more messages remain. The agent thinks it is caught up.

## Solution

After displaying messages with --mark-read, if the channel still has unread messages beyond what was shown, append a summary line including:
1. Count of remaining unread messages
2. The exact CLI command to run again (reconstructed from the original flags)

Text format:
```
--- 150 more unread messages remaining ---
advice: run `bus inbox --channels a,b,c --mentions --mark-read` to see more
```

TOON format:
```
remaining_unread: 150
has_more: true
advice: run `bus inbox --channels a,b,c --mentions --mark-read` to see more
```

JSON format:
```json
{"remaining_unread": 150, "has_more": true, "advice": "run `bus inbox --channels a,b,c --mentions --mark-read` to see more"}
```

The advice command should mirror the flags the user actually passed, so it is copy-pasteable.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] When --mark-read shows fewer messages than are available, output indicates remaining count
- [ ] Output includes the exact command to run again based on original flags
- [ ] Works across all output formats (toon/json/text)
- [ ] When all messages are shown (no more remaining), no extra output needed
- [ ] Agents can programmatically detect has_more to loop until caught up
close_reason: Completed - merged via cr-1u4c

title: Support !flags in Telegram messages to control hook behavior
description:
Support !flags in message bodies to control hook behavior. Flags are parsed and stripped from the message body before storage. Works for ALL message sources (CLI, Telegram bridge, etc).

## Flags

- `!nohooks` — Suppress all hooks (equivalent to `--no-hooks` CLI flag)
- `!nochanhooks` — Suppress channel hooks only (hooks triggered by channel messages)
- `!noathooks` — Suppress @mention hooks only (hooks triggered by @mentions)

## Behavior

1. When a message body contains a !flag, strip it from the body before storing
2. Apply the flag effect during hook evaluation
3. Flags can appear anywhere in the message body
4. Multiple flags can be combined: `hello !nochanhooks !noathooks`
5. Case-insensitive: `!NoHooks` == `!nohooks`

## Why

The CLI has `--no-hooks` but Telegram users cant pass CLI flags. !flags give Telegram users (and any message source) a way to control hook behavior inline. Also useful for agents who want to suppress specific hook types without suppressing all hooks.

## Examples

```
# From Telegram or CLI — suppress all hooks
test message !nohooks

# Suppress only channel hooks, @mention hooks still fire  
@agent-name please review !nochanhooks

# Suppress only mention hooks, channel hooks still fire
general update for the team @everyone !noathooks
```

## Implementation

1. Parse !flags from message body (regex or simple string scan)
2. Strip matched flags from body, trim whitespace
3. Store parsed flags on the Message struct (or pass as context during hook evaluation)
4. In hook evaluation code, check flags before firing

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] !nohooks suppresses all hooks
- [ ] !nochanhooks suppresses channel hooks only
- [ ] !noathooks suppresses @mention hooks only
- [ ] Flags are stripped from stored message body
- [ ] Works from CLI, Telegram, and any future message source
- [ ] Case-insensitive matching
- [ ] Multiple flags can be combined
close_reason: Completed: feat(hooks) added !flags support for inline hook control

title: Allow --mention with --claim/--ttl for hooks
description:
## Problem

Currently `bus hooks add` doesn't allow combining `--mention` with `--claim`:

```
bus hooks add --mention "agent" --claim "respond://agent" --ttl 300 ...
error: the argument '--mention <MENTION>' cannot be used with '--claim <CLAIM>'
```

## Use Case

The respond hook for conversational @mentions needs:
1. Fire on @mention (trigger condition)
2. Take a claim with TTL (prevent re-spawning while agent is active)
3. Allow the spawned script to refresh the claim if it wants to wait longer

Without this, mention-based hooks can't prevent duplicate spawns when follow-up messages arrive.

## Proposed Solution

Allow `--mention` to work with `--ttl` and `--claim-owner`. The claim pattern could be auto-derived from the mention (e.g., `respond://<agent>`) or explicitly specified.

## Workaround

title: Add requireFlag field to hooks
description:
Add a `requireFlag` field to Hook struct so hooks can opt-in to only firing when a specific message flag is present.

## Context

Dev-loop hooks (claim_available on project channels) currently fire on EVERY message — release announcements, agent chatter, casual conversation all trigger dev loops. Agents have to use `--no-hooks` to avoid this.

## Design

- Add `require_flag: Option<String>` to Hook struct
- During hook evaluation, if `require_flag` is set, skip the hook unless the message contains that flag
- Primary use case: dev-loop hooks set `requireFlag: "dev"` so they only fire on messages containing `!dev`
- The `!flags` parsing infrastructure already exists (flags.rs)
- CLI: `bus hooks add --require-flag dev ...`

## Why

This enables a tiered response model: respond hooks (mention-based) do lightweight triage, and only escalate to dev-loop via `!dev` when actual work is needed. Agents chatting in channels no longer accidentally trigger dev loops.

## Migration

Existing hooks continue to work (field is optional, defaults to None = current behavior). New dev-loop hooks should be created with `--require-flag dev`.
close_reason: Completed: require_flag field added to hooks

title: Add channel hooks: auto-spawn agents on bus send
description:
## Summary

Channel hooks: when a message is sent to a channel, evaluate conditions and optionally execute a command. Enables auto-spawning agents when work arrives.

## API Design

### Adding hooks

Command is a vector (everything after `--`). No shell expansion, direct execvp.

```
bus hooks add \
  --channel botbus \
  --if-claim-available "agent://botbus-dev" \
  --cwd /home/bob/src/botbus \
  --cooldown 30s \
  -- bash scripts/dev-loop.sh botbus botbus-dev
```

Options:
- `--channel <channel>` — which channel triggers this hook
- `--if-claim-available <claim>` — only fire if this claim URI is not held
- `--cwd <directory>` — working directory for the command (validated at add-time)
- `--cooldown <duration>` — minimum time between firings (default 30s)
- `--` — everything after this is the command vector

### Managing hooks

Each hook gets a terse ID with `hk` prefix (e.g. `hk-12d`). Will migrate to terseid library when available. Convention: prefer at least 2-char prefixes for all ID namespaces.

```
bus hooks list                  # show all configured hooks
bus hooks remove <hook-id>      # delete a hook
bus hooks test <hook-id>        # dry-run: evaluate condition, show what would execute
```

### Execution flow (on `bus send`)

1. Write the message (as normal)
2. Check if any hooks match the target channel
3. For each matching hook, evaluate the condition (`--if-claim-available`)
4. Check cooldown — skip if fired too recently
5. If condition passes, spawn command in background (fire-and-forget)
6. Append to audit log

## Security Requirements

**Critical: this is arbitrary command execution triggered by messages.**

1. **No content interpolation.** Message body, agent name, channel name are NEVER interpolated into the command. The command vector is static — exactly what was specified at add-time.
2. **Command stored as vector, not string.** No shell interpretation. Executed via direct execvp, not `sh -c`.
3. **Cooldown is mandatory.** Default 30s. The `--if-claim-available` guard is also a natural rate limiter (spawned process takes the claim, preventing re-fire).
4. **Hooks are local-only config.** Stored in `$BOTBUS_DATA_DIR/hooks/`, not in channel metadata. Two machines watching the same channel can have completely different hooks. Only the local user can add hooks.
5. **`--cwd` validated at add-time.** Directory must exist and be accessible when the hook is created.
6. **Audit log.** Every hook evaluation appended to `$BOTBUS_DATA_DIR/hooks/audit.log`: timestamp, channel, message ID, hook ID, condition result, whether command was executed, exit code (if available).

## Storage

Hook config in `$BOTBUS_DATA_DIR/hooks/`:
- `hooks.toml` or `hooks.json` — hook definitions indexed by hook ID
- `audit.log` — append-only fire log

## Future (phase 2)

- `--if-no-agent <name>` — higher-level predicate: fire if no agent with this name has been active recently. Simpler than knowing the claim URI convention.
- General hooks with more trigger types (e.g., on-claim, on-release)
- `bus daemon` / `bus watch --auto-spawn` for liveness monitoring and crash recovery
- Migrate hook IDs to terseid library (prefix: `hk`)

## Acceptance Criteria

- `bus hooks add --channel <ch> --if-claim-available <claim> --cwd <dir> -- <cmd...>` persists hook config
- Hook gets a terse ID with `hk` prefix (e.g. `hk-12d`)
- `bus hooks list` shows all hooks with IDs, channels, conditions, commands
- `bus hooks remove <id>` deletes a hook
- `bus hooks test <id>` dry-runs condition evaluation without executing
- `bus send` to a hooked channel evaluates hooks and spawns if conditions pass
- Spawn is background (sender not blocked)
- Every hook evaluation logged to audit.log
- Command executed as vector via execvp, no shell interpretation
- Cooldown enforced (default 30s)
- `--cwd` validated at add-time
close_reason: Implemented channel hooks: add, list, remove, test subcommands with claim-available condition and audit logging

title: enhance `bus channels rename`
description:
The current `bus channels rename` (channels.rs:320-415) updates state maps, FTS index, and the JSONL filename, but misses several references. This causes silent breakage for agents.

## Already handled
- Channel JSONL file (renamed)
- state.json: channel_offsets, last_seen, index_offsets, closed_channels
- FTS messages table (UPDATE channel column)

## Missing updates (high priority)
1. **Hooks** (hooks.jsonl) — hooks have a `channel: String` field. Hooks on the old channel name won't fire after rename. Since hooks.jsonl is append-only, need to append updated hook records (or rewrite).
2. **Agent subscriptions** (agents/{name}/state.json) — `subscribed_channels: Vec<String>` per agent. Agents lose their subscription to the renamed channel. Must iterate all agent state files in the agents/ directory.
3. **Agent read cursors** (agents/{name}/state.json) — `read_offsets`, `last_read_ids`, `last_read_times` are all keyed by channel name. Agents lose their read position. Same iteration as subscriptions.
4. **FTS sync_state table** (index.sqlite) — `channel TEXT PRIMARY KEY` in sync_state. The sync offset for the old channel becomes orphaned. Must DELETE old + INSERT new (can't UPDATE a PRIMARY KEY easily).

## Missing updates (medium priority)
5. **Telegram config** (~/.cache/botbus/telegram.json) — `channel_topics: HashMap<String, i64>` maps channel names to Telegram topic IDs. Bridge breaks if channel is mapped. Update key in the HashMap.

## Acceptable as-is
6. **Messages inside JSONL** — old messages retain the original channel name in their `channel` field. This is fine for append-only semantics. Search results already get the channel column updated in FTS. The message field is a historical record.

## Implementation notes
- Hooks.jsonl is append-only. Options: (a) append new hook records with updated channel + deactivation records for old hooks, or (b) rewrite the file. Option (b) is simpler since hooks.jsonl is small and already fully loaded into memory during evaluation.
- Agent state files are regular JSON (not append-only), so they can be loaded, modified, and saved.
- Add `--yes`/`-y` flag for scripted use (consistent with `messages delete`).

## Files
- `src/cli/channels.rs:320-415` — rename() function (extend this)
- `src/core/hook.rs` — Hook struct, hooks_path()
- `src/storage/agent_state.rs` — AgentState struct, per-agent state files
- `src/index/fts.rs:155-177` — sync_state table access
- `src/index/schema.rs` — sync_state schema
- `src/telegram/config.rs` — TelegramConfig, channel_topics map

title: channels rename: update agent state files
description:
When `bus channels rename old new` runs, all per-agent state files must have channel references updated.

## Context
Each agent has a state file at `~/.local/share/botbus/agents/{agent_name}/state.json` (storage/agent_state.rs). These contain:
- `subscribed_channels: Vec<String>` (line 32) — agents lose subscription
- `read_offsets: HashMap<String, u64>` (line 20) — read position lost
- `last_read_ids: HashMap<String, String>` (line 24) — last read ID lost
- `last_read_times: HashMap<String, DateTime<Utc>>` (line 28) — last read time lost

All are keyed/indexed by channel name. After rename, agents lose their read position and subscriptions.

## Implementation
In `channels.rs:rename()`, after updating global state:
1. List all agent directories under the agents/ dir (`agents_dir()` or similar)
2. For each agent state file, load it via AgentState
3. Rename channel key in `read_offsets`, `last_read_ids`, `last_read_times` HashMaps
4. Replace old channel name in `subscribed_channels` Vec
5. Save updated state
6. Print `✓ Updated N agent state(s)` (or skip if none affected)

Agent state files are regular JSON (not append-only), so load-modify-save is fine.

- `src/cli/channels.rs:320-415` — rename() function
- `src/storage/agent_state.rs` — AgentState struct, load/save methods
- `src/core/project.rs` — agents dir path helper (may need to add one)

title: Mention hooks auto-derive respond:// claim pattern, bypassing claim exclusivity
description:
## Problem

When a `--mention` hook has a `claim_release` (via `--ttl` or `--release-on-exit`), the hook evaluation code auto-derives a `respond://<agent>` claim pattern. This is a different namespace from the `agent://<agent>` pattern used by `--claim` hooks, so both hooks fire simultaneously for the same agent — defeating claim exclusivity.

Example: `hk-e0m` (ClaimAvailable, pattern `agent://botcrit-dev`) and `hk-gtn` (MentionReceived for `botcrit-dev`, auto-deriving `respond://botcrit-dev`) both fire when `@botcrit-dev` is mentioned on channel `#botcrit`.

## Root Cause

`evaluate_hooks_inner()` auto-derived `respond://<agent>` as the claim pattern for MentionReceived hooks. This never conflicted with `agent://` patterns from ClaimAvailable hooks.

Additionally, `--claim` and `--mention` were mutually exclusive in the CLI (`conflicts_with`), so there was no way to give a mention hook an explicit claim pattern.

## Fix

1. Remove auto-derived `respond://` claim pattern from mention hooks
2. Allow `--mention` and `--claim` to be combined — the user specifies the exact claim pattern
3. Add `claim_pattern` field to Hook struct for mention+claim hooks
4. Mention hooks without `--claim` fire without any claim acquisition (simple fire-on-mention)

## Status

Fixed in this commit.

title: Use BOTBUS_AGENT env var for identity instead of state file
description:

title: TUI: add 'New Messages' separator that auto-clears after viewing
description:
## Overview

Add a visual separator in the TUI message view to clearly indicate where new (unread) messages begin. The separator should:
- Display between old and new messages
- Use Unicode box drawing characters (─) with centered text
- Auto-clear after 2 seconds of viewing the messages
- Clear the (x) new messages indicator when dismissed

## Design

### Visual Example

```
[10:30] alice: Old message 1
[10:31] bob: Old message 2
─────────── New Messages ───────────
[10:45] charlie: New message 1
[10:46] dave: New message 2
```

### Behavior

1. **Show separator when:**
   - Channel has new messages (count > 0)
   - User switches to a channel with new messages
   - New messages arrive in current channel

2. **Position:**
   - Between last "old" message and first "new" message
   - Based on initial_sizes tracking

3. **Auto-clear:**
   - Start 2-second timer when channel with separator is viewed
   - After 2 seconds, remove separator and reset new message count to 0
   - Update initial_sizes to current size (mark all as read)

4. **Manual clear:**
   - User can press 'm' to manually mark all as read (existing behavior?)
   - Or just wait 2 seconds

## Implementation Notes

### Data Model Changes

In `src/tui/app.rs`:
```rust
pub struct App {
    // Existing fields...
    
    /// Timestamp when current channel was focused (for auto-clear timer)
    channel_focused_at: Option<std::time::Instant>,
    
    /// Which channels have visible separators
    separator_visible: std::collections::HashSet<String>,
}
```

### Render Logic

In message rendering (`src/tui/ui.rs`):
1. Get new message count for current channel
2. If count > 0, find separator position (based on initial_sizes)
3. Insert separator line at that position
4. Style: dimmed gray box characters with centered bold text

### Timer Logic

In event loop (`src/tui/app.rs::run_loop`):
1. When channel selected, set `channel_focused_at = Some(Instant::now())`
2. Each loop iteration, check if 2 seconds elapsed
3. If elapsed and separator visible, clear it:
   - Remove from `separator_visible`
   - Update `initial_sizes[channel]` to current size
   - Clear `new_message_counts[channel]`

### Unicode Characters

Use box drawing characters:
- Horizontal line: `─` (U+2500)
- Alternative: `━` (U+2501) for thicker line

Center text by calculating padding based on terminal width.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Separator appears between old and new messages
- [ ] Separator uses Unicode box drawing with centered text
- [ ] Separator auto-clears after 2 seconds of viewing
- [ ] New message indicator (x) clears when separator dismissed
- [ ] Separator doesn't appear if all messages are new
- [ ] Works correctly when switching between channels

## Future Enhancements (out of scope)

- Configurable timer duration
- Option to disable auto-clear
- Different styles for separator (color, thickness)
close_reason: Implemented new messages separator with auto-clear

title: Design: Message conventions and best practices
description:
Design message conventions and best practices for agent communication.

## Core Principle
Messages should be concise, actionable, and provide context for async coordination.
Feel should be like IRC - casual, lowercase, direct.

## Message Patterns

### Status Updates
```
starting bd-xyz: brief description of what you're working on
progress bd-xyz: 3/5 tests passing, debugging edge case
done bd-xyz: implemented feature, all tests passing
```

### Coordination
```
blocked: need api credentials to proceed with bd-xyz
question: should auth middleware go in src/api or src/auth?
handoff @swift-raven: bd-xyz ready for review
```

### Cross-Project
```
fyi: released botbus v0.2.0, may affect botcrit integration
@crimson-fox can you help with the database schema?
```

## Example Conversation (IRC style)
```
#botbus
<quiet-river> starting bd-123: tui scrolling fix
<swift-raven> sounds good, i'll handle bd-124 (cli cleanup)
<quiet-river> question: should scroll speed be configurable?
<swift-raven> probably not for now, keep it simple
<quiet-river> done bd-123: fixed scrolling, tests pass
<swift-raven> nice, i'll review after finishing bd-124
```

## Labels (Optional Feature)
Consider supporting labels for filtering:
```bash
botbus send botbus "fixed bug" --label=bug --label=done
botbus history --label=blocked
```

Labels: bug, feature, question, blocked, done, review, urgent

## Documentation Locations
1. AGENTS.md template - quick reference
2. README.md - overview with examples
3. `botbus send --help` - inline examples
close_reason: Message conventions documented in AGENTS.md

title: TUI doesn't show new channels created while watching
description:
## Problem

When `botbus ui` is running and a new channel is created (e.g., another agent runs `botbus send new-channel "hello"`), the TUI doesn't show the new channel in the channel list. It only appears after restarting the TUI.

## Expected Behavior

The TUI should automatically detect and display new channels when they're created while watching.

## Current Behavior

- TUI starts and shows existing channels
- Another agent creates a new channel
- TUI continues showing only the original channels
- Need to quit and restart TUI to see the new channel

## Reproduction Steps

```bash
# Terminal 1
export BOTBUS_AGENT=agent-1
botbus ui

# Terminal 2
export BOTBUS_AGENT=agent-2
botbus send test-channel-new "hello"

# Back to Terminal 1 - test-channel-new doesn't appear in channel list
```

## Technical Details

The TUI uses `storage::watch::watch_directory()` to monitor the channels directory, but likely:
1. Only watches for modifications to existing channel files
2. Doesn't watch for new files being created in the directory
3. Or the watch events aren't triggering a channel list refresh

## Suggested Fix

In `src/tui/app.rs`:
- Ensure file watcher monitors for CREATE events on channels directory
- When CREATE event detected, refresh the channel list
- Update `App::refresh_channels()` or equivalent to reload from disk

Files to check:
- `src/storage/watch.rs` - watch implementation
- `src/tui/app.rs` - event handling and channel list management
close_reason: Fixed - TUI now refreshes channel list on CREATE events

title: TUI agents pane doesn't show spawned agents
description:
## Problem
Spawned agents (like hidden-tide, jasper-mountain, fierce-falcon) don't appear in the TUI's Agents pane at all. The agents pane only shows agents that have set `agent://` claims.

## Root Cause
The `update_agent_statuses()` function in `src/tui/app.rs` discovers agents from two sources:
1. `agent://` claims in claims.jsonl
2. Status entries in statuses.jsonl

However, spawned worker agents typically set `bead://` and `workspace://` claims but never set `agent://` claims. They also may not set status entries.

## Expected Behavior
The TUI should show agents discovered from message history (like `bus agents` CLI does), or from any claim type (bead://, workspace://, etc.), not just `agent://` claims.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Spawned agents appear in TUI agents pane after sending messages
- [ ] Agents discovered from any claim type show in the pane
- [ ] Online/Afk/Offline status is correctly determined

## Testing Strategy
1. Spawn a worker agent (or simulate with `bus send --agent test-worker botbus "Hello"`)
2. Verify the agent appears in TUI agents pane
3. Verify status updates correctly based on activity

comments.author: botbus-dev
comments.text:
## Grooming Notes

### Current State
- **OutputFormat enum**: 3 variants — Text, Json, Toon (default)
- **TOON**: Custom flat key:value format in format.rs. Used as default for all commands.
- **JSON wrapping**: Most commands already wrap arrays in named objects (e.g. `{ "claims": [...] }`). Exception: `statuses list` returns bare array.
- **advice field**: Only `inbox` has it (as `Option<String>`). The botbox convention uses `advice: []` (array of strings).
- **Pretty format**: Does not exist.
- **TTY detection**: None — no env var, no auto-detection.
- **Format inconsistencies**: `search` and `wait` use `--json` boolean flag instead of `--format`.

### Scope Analysis (13+ commands with format support)
Commands needing updates: doctor, whoami, history, channels list, agents, claims list/check, inbox, status, hooks add/list/remove/test, statuses set/clear/list, messages get, sync status/log/check, search, wait.

### Recommended Epic Structure

**Phase 1: Format infrastructure**
- Drop Toon variant, add Pretty variant to OutputFormat enum
- TTY auto-detection: `--format flag > FORMAT env > TTY→pretty / non-TTY→text`
- Pretty = colored, human-friendly output (what Text does today with colored crate)
- Text = concise, no color, agent-friendly (ID-first, two-space delimiters)
- Add `advice: Vec<String>` to all JSON output structs (standardize on array, not Option<String>)
- Remove format.rs TOON implementation, update tests
- Update `--json` deprecated flag to keep working

**Phase 2: Per-command text output standardization**
- Audit each command's Text output for: ID-first records, two-space delimiters, no prose, self-labeling
- Migrate `search` and `wait` from `--json` bool to `--format` enum
- Fix `statuses list` bare array JSON

### Risks
- **Breaking change for agents**: Dropping TOON as default will change output for all agents that don't specify `--format`. Mitigate: make text output agent-friendly by design.
- **Large surface area**: 13+ commands × 3 format branches = 39+ code paths to update.
- **Advice content**: Need to define what advice strings to include for each command. Should be contextual 'next step' commands.

### Recommendation
Convert to epic with 3-4 children. Phase 1 (infrastructure) blocks Phase 2 (per-command).

title: Integration tests for git sync in isolated /tmp environment
description:
Write integration tests for the git sync feature using isolated temp directories via BOTBUS_DATA_DIR.

## Test infrastructure

Use existing TestProject harness in tests/common/mod.rs. It already:
- Creates temp dirs via tempfile::TempDir
- Sets BOTBUS_DATA_DIR for process isolation
- Provides Agent helpers for running commands
- Has BotbusOutput with assert helpers

## Test file

Create tests/sync.rs with integration tests.

## Test scenarios

### 1. bus sync init
- Init creates .git/, .gitattributes, .gitignore in data dir
- .gitattributes contains "*.jsonl merge=union"
- .gitignore contains *.db, state.json, attachments/
- Init with --remote configures the remote
- Init on already-initialized dir is idempotent or errors gracefully

### 2. Auto-commit after operations
- After bus send: git log shows commit for the message
- After bus claims stake: git log shows commit for the claim
- After bus claims release: git log shows commit for the release
- Without sync init (no .git): operations still succeed (no auto-commit, no error)

### 3. Two-machine sync simulation
- Create two TestProjects (machine A, machine B)
- Init git in both, connect via bare repo as remote
- Machine A sends messages, pushes
- Machine B pulls, sees Machine A messages
- Machine B sends messages, pushes
- Machine A pulls, sees Machine B messages
- Verify union merge deduplicates correctly (no duplicate ULIDs)

### 4. Index rebuild
- Send messages, init sync, rebuild index
- Verify search still works after rebuild
- Verify --if-needed skips when index is current
- Verify bus index status reports correctly

### 5. bus sync status / check / log
- sync status shows clean state after init
- sync log shows commits
- sync check reports health correctly

## Acceptance criteria
- All tests pass in complete isolation (no touching ~/.local/share/botbus)
- Tests use BOTBUS_DATA_DIR for all data
- Two-machine simulation uses git bare repo as intermediary
- Tests are added to tests/sync.rs

title: --attach parsing breaks on colons in filenames
description:
`bus send --attach` uses `split_once(":")` to detect `name:path` syntax. This breaks when the path itself contains colons (common in timestamp-based filenames like screenshots).

## Repro

```bash
bus send general "test" --attach ~/shots/2026-02-05_14:57:33.png
# Error: Attachment file not found: 57:33.png
```

The parser sees `~/shots/2026-02-05_14` as the name and `57:33.png` as the path.

## Location

src/cli/send.rs:219 — `split_once(":")`

## Fix options

1. Only treat as `name:path` if the part before `:` does NOT contain `/` or `\` (a name shouldnt be a path)
2. Check if the whole spec exists as a file first, fall back to name:path parsing only if it doesnt
3. Use a different separator (e.g. `=` instead of `:`)

Option 2 is safest — try the whole string as a path first, only split on `:` as fallback.
close_reason: Fixed: try whole spec as path before splitting on colon

title: support deleting messages
description:
Add `bus messages delete <id>` command that tombstones messages in append-only JSONL.

## Motivation
The #projects channel is used as a registry and has junk entries that need cleanup.

## Design
- **Tombstone approach**: Append a new JSONL record with `MessageMeta::Deleted { target_id: Ulid, deleted_by: String, deleted_at: DateTime<Utc> }` keyed by the original message's ULID. The tombstone itself gets its own ULID. No in-place edits to JSONL.
- **Interactive confirmation**: Follow the channel delete pattern (channels.rs:252-313) — warn, prompt for typed confirmation. Add `--yes`/`-y` flag for scripted/batch use.
- **Display**: TUI shows `[message deleted]` in dim italic (like system messages). `bus messages get` shows deletion metadata. Search excludes deleted messages.
- **Sync**: Works naturally — deletion record is just another append, replicated by git sync. Eventual consistency: remote machines see deletion after next sync.

## Key architectural decision: read_messages() wrapper
The **filtering surface area is wide** — history, inbox, search, wait, watch, TUI, agents, status all read messages. Missing one means deleted messages leak through.

Add a `read_messages(path) -> Vec<Message>` wrapper around `read_records::<Message>(path)` that:
1. Collects all tombstone `target_id`s into a HashSet
2. Filters out both the tombstone records AND the original deleted messages
3. Returns only live messages

Use this wrapper everywhere instead of raw `read_records::<Message>`. Single filtering point prevents leaks.

## Implementation
1. Add `MessageMeta::Deleted { target_id: Ulid, deleted_by: String, deleted_at: DateTime<Utc> }` variant to `core/message.rs`
2. Add `read_messages(path)` wrapper to `storage/jsonl.rs` (or `core/message.rs`) — two-pass: collect tombstone IDs, then filter
3. Replace all `read_records::<Message>` call sites with `read_messages` (grep for these — history, inbox, agents, status, TUI, watch, wait, search)
4. Add `Delete { id: String }` to `MessagesCommands` in `cli/mod.rs`, implement in `cli/messages.rs`
5. Find message across all channels by scanning JSONL files (same as `messages get`), append tombstone to same channel file
6. Update `tui/ui.rs` `format_message()` — early return for Deleted variant with dim styling
7. Update `index/fts.rs` — incremental sync must DELETE from FTS index when encountering tombstone records (not just skip)
8. Update `cli/messages.rs` `get()` to handle deleted messages gracefully

## Watch out for
- **Incremental index sync**: Tombstone records advance byte offsets. The indexer must remove originals from FTS, not just skip. This is a different operation than it currently does.
- **Read performance**: O(2n) per channel read due to two-pass filtering. Fine at botbus scale.
- **DM channels**: `_dm_agent1_agent2` — deletion should work the same, test explicitly.

## Files
- `src/core/message.rs` — MessageMeta enum + read_messages wrapper
- `src/storage/jsonl.rs` — possibly home for read_messages
- `src/cli/mod.rs` — MessagesCommands
- `src/cli/messages.rs` — delete + get changes
- `src/cli/send.rs` — uses read_records for history
- `src/cli/agents.rs` — derives agents from messages
- `src/cli/status.rs` — reads messages for last_seen
- `src/cli/inbox.rs` — reads messages
- `src/cli/history.rs` — reads messages
- `src/tui/app.rs` — TUI message loading
- `src/tui/ui.rs` — format_message display
- `src/index/fts.rs` — search filtering + incremental sync
- `src/index/sync.rs` — index sync
- `src/main.rs` — dispatcher
close_reason: done

title: channels rename: update FTS sync_state table
description:
When `bus channels rename old new` runs, the FTS sync_state table in index.sqlite must be updated.

## Context
The sync_state table (index/schema.rs:4-21) has `channel TEXT PRIMARY KEY` with an offset and last_sync timestamp. After rename, the old channel entry becomes orphaned and the indexer won't know the byte offset for the renamed channel.

The current rename already updates the `messages` FTS table (channels.rs:393-396) but misses sync_state.

## Implementation
In `channels.rs:rename()`, in the existing index update block (after line 396):
1. Delete old sync_state row: `DELETE FROM sync_state WHERE channel = ?`
2. Insert new row: `INSERT INTO sync_state (channel, offset, last_sync) SELECT ?, offset, last_sync FROM ...`
   - Or simpler: read old row values first, delete, insert with new channel name
3. Print as part of existing `✓ Updated search index` message

Since channel is a PRIMARY KEY, UPDATE may not work cleanly across all SQLite versions. DELETE + INSERT is safest.

## Files
- `src/cli/channels.rs:388-398` — existing index update block in rename()
- `src/index/schema.rs:4-21` — sync_state schema
- `src/index/fts.rs:155-177` — get/set_sync_offset methods

## Testing
- Index a channel, rename it, verify `bus index status` shows correct state
- Verify incremental index sync works after rename (doesn't re-index everything)

title: JSONL files should handle concurrent writes safely
description:
When multiple agents run in parallel (via maw workspaces), they all write to the same botbus JSONL files:
- .botbus/agents.jsonl
- .botbus/channels/general.jsonl
- .botbus/claims.jsonl

This causes merge conflicts when the workspaces are merged:
```
.botbus/agents.jsonl              3-sided conflict
.botbus/channels/general.jsonl    3-sided conflict
.botbus/claims.jsonl              3-sided conflict
```

Since these are append-only logs, the resolution is always 'keep all entries sorted by timestamp', but this currently requires manual intervention.

**Possible solutions:**

1. **File locking** - Use advisory locks (like fs2 crate) when appending to JSONL files, similar to how botcrit's AppendLog works. This would prevent concurrent writes from creating conflicts in the first place.

2. **Agent-specific files** - Each agent writes to its own file (e.g., .botbus/agents/AgentDrift.jsonl), and botbus aggregates them when reading. No conflicts possible.

3. **Accept conflicts, provide merge tool** - Add a 'botbus merge' command that knows how to resolve these JSONL conflicts automatically (concatenate and sort by timestamp).

**Discovered in:** botcrit multi-agent coordination with 3 parallel agents
close_reason: File locking already implemented. The actual issue is git merge conflicts when using worktrees/workspaces - addressed by bd-1o1 (migration to global storage)

title: Design: Channel naming conventions for multi-project
description:
Design channel naming conventions for multi-project global BotBus.

## Decision: Flat namespace with dot hierarchy

Channels are simple strings. Convention (not enforcement) provides structure.

## Channel Types
```
general              - Cross-project coordination (always exists)
<project>            - Project-specific (e.g., botbus, botcrit)
<project>.<topic>    - Sub-topics (e.g., botbus.tui, botcrit.api)
_dm_<A>_<B>          - Direct messages (system-managed, names sorted)
```

## Validation Rules
- Lowercase alphanumeric + hyphens + dots
- No leading/trailing dots or hyphens
- No consecutive dots
- Max length: 64 chars
- Must start with letter (except _ for system channels)

## System Channels (reserved prefix: _)
- `_dm_*` - Direct message channels
- Future: `_system` for botbus internal messages

## Auto-Creation
- Channels are created on first message (no explicit create command)
- `general` channel is pre-created on first botbus command (ensure_data_dir)

## Commands
```bash
botbus channels              # List all channels with activity stats
botbus history               # Recent messages across all channels  
botbus history <channel>     # Messages in specific channel
botbus send <channel> <msg>  # Send to channel (creates if needed)
botbus send @Agent <msg>     # DM (creates _dm_ channel)
```

## Examples
```bash
botbus send general 'Hello everyone'
botbus send botbus 'Working on TUI feature'
botbus send botbus.tui 'Fixed scrolling bug'
botbus send @CrimsonFox 'Can you review this?'
```

## Project Name Convention
Agents should use the git repo name or directory name as the project channel:
- Working in ~/src/botbus -> send to #botbus
- Working in ~/src/my-cool-app -> send to #my-cool-app

This is convention only - botbus doesn't enforce or auto-detect.

title: Add advice[] array to all JSON output structs
description:
Standardize on `advice: Vec<String>` (not Option<String>) in all JSON output structs. The advice array contains suggested next commands, contextual to the operation performed.

Pattern (from maw/botbox convention):
```json
{
  "claims": [...],
  "advice": ["bus claims release --all"]
}
```

Commands to update (all output structs):
- ChannelsOutput (channels.rs)
- ClaimsOutput, ClaimCheckOutput (claim.rs)
- AgentsOutput (agents.rs)
- HistoryOutput (history.rs)
- InboxOutput — already has advice but as Option<String>, change to Vec<String>
- MentionsOutput (inbox.rs)
- StatusOutput (status.rs)
- HookOutput structs (hooks.rs)
- StatusesOutput (statuses.rs) — also fix bare array to wrapped object
- SyncStatusOutput, SyncLogOutput (sync commands)
- DoctorOutput (doctor.rs)
- SearchOutput (search.rs)
- WaitOutput (wait.rs)
- MessagesOutput (messages.rs)

Advice content should be contextual — e.g. after `claims list`, suggest `bus claims release --all` if agent has active claims. After `inbox`, suggest `bus mark-read <channel>`. Empty array is fine when no obvious next step.

Use `#[serde(skip_serializing_if = "Vec::is_empty")]` to keep JSON clean when no advice.

title: channels rename: update hooks on rename
description:
When `bus channels rename old new` runs, hooks referencing the old channel name must be updated.

## Context
Hooks are stored in hooks.jsonl (append-only). Each hook has a `channel: String` field (core/hook.rs). After rename, hooks on the old channel silently stop firing.

## Implementation
In `channels.rs:rename()`, after updating state:
1. Read all hooks via `read_records::<Hook>(&hooks_path())`
2. Find hooks where `hook.channel == old_name`
3. Rewrite hooks.jsonl with updated channel names (hooks.jsonl is small, fully loaded into memory during evaluation already — rewrite is simpler than append-based updates)
4. Print `✓ Updated N hook(s)` (or skip message if none matched)

## Files
- `src/cli/channels.rs:320-415` — rename() function
- `src/core/hook.rs` — Hook struct, hooks_path()
- `src/storage/jsonl.rs` — read_records, write helpers

## Testing
- Create a hook on a channel, rename the channel, verify the hook fires on the new name
- Verify hooks on other channels are unaffected
close_reason: done

title: Docs: Update AGENTS.md with conventions
description:
Update AGENTS.md template and generation with new conventions.

## Key Changes
- Remove registration instructions
- Add BOTBUS_AGENT setup guidance  
- Document channel naming conventions
- Document message conventions
- Document claim workflow
- Use kebab-case agent names in examples

## Template Content

```markdown
## BotBus Agent Communication

This project uses BotBus for agent coordination. BotBus is installed globally at ~/.local/share/botbus/

### Setup

Set your agent identity (required for all commands):
\`\`\`bash
# Generate a random name
export BOTBUS_AGENT=$(botbus generate-name)

# Or choose your own (kebab-case preferred)
export BOTBUS_AGENT=my-agent-name
\`\`\`

### Quick Reference
\`\`\`bash
botbus send general "Cross-project message"
botbus send projectname "Project-specific message"
botbus send @agent-name "Direct message"

botbus claim "src/**/*.rs" -m "Working on refactor"
botbus release --all

botbus history
botbus agents
botbus claims
\`\`\`

### Channel Conventions
- \`general\` - Cross-project coordination
- \`<project>\` - Project-specific (e.g., botbus, botcrit)
- \`<project>.<topic>\` - Sub-topics (e.g., botbus.tui)
- \`@agent-name\` - Direct messages

### Message Conventions
- Starting: "Starting bd-xyz: Brief description"
- Progress: "Progress bd-xyz: Status update"  
- Blocked: "Blocked: Description of blocker"
- Question: "Question: Your question here"
- Done: "Done bd-xyz: What was completed"

### File Claims
Claim files before editing to signal intent:
\`\`\`bash
botbus claim "src/api/**" -m "Working on API refactor"
botbus check-claim src/api/routes.rs  # Check before editing
botbus release --all                   # When done
\`\`\`
```

title: Fix telegram system message filter to include claims
description:
The telegram service's is_system_message() only filters MessageMeta::System, but misses Claim, ClaimExtended, and Release messages. These messages are being published to Telegram when they shouldn't be.

**Root cause**: src/telegram/service.rs:637-639 only checks for System variant:
```rust
fn is_system_message(msg: &Message) -> bool {
    matches\!(&msg.meta, Some(MessageMeta::System { .. }))
}
```

**Expected**: Should also filter Claim, ClaimExtended, and Release like the TUI does (src/tui/ui.rs:428-431).

**Fix**: Update the match to include all four variants:
```rust
fn is_system_message(msg: &Message) -> bool {
    matches\!(&msg.meta, Some(
        MessageMeta::System { .. } | 
        MessageMeta::Claim { .. } | 
        MessageMeta::ClaimExtended { .. } | 
        MessageMeta::Release { .. }
    ))
}
```

**Acceptance criteria**:
- Claim, ClaimExtended, and Release messages are NOT published to Telegram
- System messages remain filtered
- Tests pass
close_reason: Completed - extended is_system_message() to filter Claim, ClaimExtended, and Release message types

title: TOON format is less token-efficient than text for inbox/history
description:
TOON format for inbox and history uses significantly more bytes/tokens than plain text, defeating its purpose as a token-efficient format for AI agents.

## Evidence

5 messages from `bus history general -n 5`:
- TOON: 1348 bytes
- Text: 608 bytes
- TOON is **2.2x larger** than text

The problem is worse for inbox because of deeply nested `messages.N.field` dot notation repeating the full path for every field of every message.

Example TOON (verbose):
```
messages.0.agent: bob
messages.0.body: hello
messages.0.channel: general
messages.0.id: 01KGJ...
messages.0.ts: 2026-02-03T19:21:00Z
messages.1.agent: alice
messages.1.body: hi
messages.1.channel: general
messages.1.id: 01KGJ...
messages.1.ts: 2026-02-03T19:22:00Z
```

Equivalent text (compact):
```
[14:21] bob: hello
[14:22] alice: hi
```

## Options to consider

1. **Change default format for inbox/history to text** — these are read-heavy commands where structured parsing is rarely needed
2. **Make TOON more compact for message lists** — e.g. use a tabular or abbreviated format instead of dot-path expansion
3. **Add a compact TOON variant** — collapse repeated prefixes
4. **Per-command format defaults** — let inbox/history default to text while status/claims default to toon

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Investigate and decide on approach
- [ ] inbox and history output should be more token-efficient for AI agents by default
- [ ] Structured output (--format json/toon) still available when explicitly requested
close_reason: Completed - merged via cr-38rr
